description: Polish sweet yeast dough (ciasto drożdżowe) is an enriched dough: flour + liquid + fat + eggs + sugar + yeast. Hydration 55-60% creates soft, tearable crumb. Critical rules: butter added AFTER gluten develops, yeast never touches salt or fat directly, windowpane test is the only reliable doneness indicator for kneading.

base_500g_flour.flour_type: mąka poznańska typ 500 — medium gluten, ideal for enriched dough
base_500g_flour.note: Extra yolk adds richness and color without excess water. Salt added to flour, never to yeast.

proportions.fat_role: Fat coats gluten strands — inhibits development if added too early. Add softened (not melted) butter only after 5 min initial kneading when gluten network has begun forming.
proportions.sugar_limit: Max 20% of flour weight (100g per 500g flour). Higher sugar dehydrates yeast cells osmotically — use osmotolerant yeast (SAF Gold) for very sweet doughs.
proportions.milk_temperature: 35-38°C exactly — use thermometer. Below 25°C: yeast dormant. Above 43°C: yeast begins dying. Above 50°C: yeast fully killed.

technique.yeast_activation: Crumble fresh yeast into warm milk (35-38°C) with 1 tsp sugar and 2 tbsp flour — whisk, wait 10-15 min until visibly foamy and doubled. If no foam after 15 min, yeast is dead — discard and start again.
technique.mixing_order: Flour + eggs + salt + yeast mixture → mix until shaggy, knead 5 min → add softened butter in 4-5 small pieces, one at a time, each piece fully incorporated before next → knead 8-12 min until smooth
technique.windowpane_test: Pinch off walnut-sized piece, stretch slowly between fingers — should stretch to translucent membrane thin enough to see light through without tearing. If it tears immediately, knead 3 more minutes and test again.
technique.first_rise: Covered with damp cloth or plastic wrap, 26-28°C, 60-90 min until exactly doubled in volume. Poke test: finger indent springs back slowly = ready. Springs back fast = needs more time. Doesn't spring back = overproofed.
technique.degassing: Fold dough gently 2-3 times (like an envelope) — do NOT punch down aggressively, this tears gluten structure
technique.shaping: Shape on lightly floured surface with minimal flour. Tight surface tension is key — tuck dough under itself to create tension on top.
technique.second_rise: In tin or on tray, covered, 26-28°C, 30-45 min. Ready when dough crowns 1.5-2cm above tin rim and slowly springs back when gently pressed with oiled finger.
technique.egg_wash: 1 egg yolk + 1 tbsp milk + pinch salt, brushed gently with soft brush just before baking — gives deep golden color
technique.baking: 180°C top-bottom heat, 30-40 min depending on size. For large loaf, cover with foil after 20 min if browning too fast.
technique.doneness: Internal temperature 88-92°C measured with thermometer. Sounds hollow when tapped on bottom. Do not rely on color alone — interior may be raw despite golden crust.
technique.cold_retard_option: After shaping, refrigerate covered overnight (4-8°C) for second rise. Bake cold from fridge next morning — develops complex flavor through slow fermentation, more convenient for morning baking.

tangzhong_technique.what: Tangzhong (湯種) is a Japanese/Chinese pre-gelatinization technique: cook 5-10% of the recipe's flour with 5x its weight in water/milk to 65°C until it becomes a thick gel. This gel is added to the main dough.
tangzhong_technique.why: At 65°C starch granules absorb water and gelatinize — this pre-absorbed water is locked inside the gel and cannot evaporate during baking. Result: bread/buns stay soft and moist for 3-4 days instead of going stale overnight.
tangzhong_technique.how: For 500g flour recipe: take 25g flour (5%) + 125ml milk/water. Whisk together cold, then heat in saucepan over medium heat, stirring constantly until mixture thickens to pudding consistency (65°C). Cool to room temperature before adding to dough.
tangzhong_technique.ratio: 5% of total flour weight for standard softness. Up to 10% for milk bread / Hokkaido style — extremely soft, pillowy crumb.
tangzhong_technique.effect_on_dough: Dough will be slightly stickier than usual — resist adding flour. The extra hydration locked in tangzhong is intentional.
tangzhong_technique.pro_note: Tangzhong is the single biggest upgrade for any enriched yeast dough. It costs 5 extra minutes and transforms the shelf life from 1 day to 3-4 days.

failure_modes.result: yeast partially or fully killed — dough rises very slowly or not at all
failure_modes.fix: always measure milk temperature with thermometer — wrist test is unreliable

failure_modes.case: butter added too early (before initial kneading)
failure_modes.result: fat coats flour particles before gluten can form — dense, crumbly, brioche-like but without structure
failure_modes.fix: knead flour+eggs+yeast+milk for minimum 5 minutes before adding any butter

failure_modes.case: butter added melted instead of softened
failure_modes.result: incorporates too quickly, coats gluten unevenly, greasy crumb
failure_modes.fix: butter must be 18-20°C — pliable but holds shape when pressed. Never melt.

failure_modes.result: poor gluten structure, dense crumb, tears easily when sliced
failure_modes.fix: always verify with windowpane test — time guidelines are secondary to the physical test

failure_modes.result: collapsed structure, sour taste, large irregular holes, collapses in oven
failure_modes.fix: watch volume not time. If overproofed: reshape, let rise again from scratch (though flavor is compromised)

failure_modes.case: too much flour added during kneading
failure_modes.result: dry, tight, dense crumb — most common beginner mistake
failure_modes.fix: enriched dough MUST be sticky at start — resist adding flour. Stickiness disappears after 8 min kneading as gluten develops.

failure_modes.result: osmotic stress kills or weakens yeast — sluggish rise
failure_modes.fix: mix salt into flour first, yeast into liquid separately

failure_modes.result: dense interior, 'flying crust' (crust separates from crumb)
failure_modes.fix: wait until dough crowns visibly above tin rim before baking

description: Fat and egg content determine dough behavior, rise time, and final texture. Richer doughs need longer fermentation, gentler handling, and lower baking temperatures to prevent over-browning before interior sets.
pro_note: The richer the dough, the more important cold retard becomes — overnight refrigeration at 4°C slows fermentation, develops flavor complexity impossible with room-temperature speed rising.
